SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: Lichen Sclerosus – the PETALS multicentre randomised controlled feasibility

APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary:
Plain English summary
Background
Vulval lichen sclerosus (VLS) is an itchy condition affecting the skin around the entrance to the vagina (vulva). In
severe cases, it causes scarring and fusion of parts of the vulva, leading to painful skin tears, difficulty in having sex
and going to the toilet. VLS affects women of any age but is most diagnosed before puberty and after menopause.
Treatment includes lifelong use of strong corticosteroid creams, which are effective for most women. For most
patients the condition is controlled by this treatment but some experience ongoing symptoms, scarring and skin
thickening. Recent studies have introduced two potential surgical treatments: fat grafting and platelet-rich plasma
which may improve VLS symptoms and reduce scarring. Fat grafting involves collecting fat from the patient’s own
body and injecting it into the vulva. Platelet-rich plasma is obtained from the patient's blood, prepared in a special
way, and then injected into the vulva.
Aims n
We want to carry out a feasibility (pilot) study in which patients will either undergo one of these new treatments or
o
use corticosteroid cream only to check if these treatments are acceptable and how to best measure their
effectiveness. We also want to know if it is possible to perform a larger trial in multiple hospitals in the future to find
out if these treatments work. i
s
Design and methods
s
This study will involve up to 75 women with VLS. They will be allocated randomly and equally into three groups: fat
grafting, platelet-rich plasma and corticosteroid cream only. Patients in the surgical groups will undergo one surgery
i
and will continue using the corticosteroid cream. Participants will complete questionnaires about their symptoms,
m
quality of life and sexual function. We will assess improvements by analysing photographs of the vulva and
measuring the thickness of the vulval skin using specialised equipment. Patients will be followed up for six months.
Patient and public involvement
b
Our patient representative, a woman with VLS, has contributed to the study design. We also conducted an online
survey involving 809 women with VLS u to shape the study. The patient representative will remain on the trial
committee and participate in decision-making throughout the study. We established a patient advisory group of
women with VLS to gather their ongoing opinions and ensure our research aligns with patients’ priorities.
s

APPLICATION DETAILS.Working with People and Communities Summ:
Please describe how patients and the public have been involved in developing this proposal.
The PPI activity during the trial design phase included one-to-one interviews, focus groups and online surveys. We
created a PAG after NIHR Stage 1 feedback. Members of the PAG have a lived experience of VLS and lipotransfer
surgery. Please see the Patient and Public section of the Detailed Research Plan for further details of the PPI
approach used during the trial design phase.
Our patient co-applicant will be the PPI lead for this study and a member of the TMG. She will coordinate and chair
regular PAG meetings and attend the TMG meeting throughout the study duration. Her involvement will be
reimbursed as per a standard PPI rate at Royal Free Hospital of £120 per day and the cost of a day return tube
ticket. All members of the PAG will be reimbursed for their involvement in the online meetings.
n
The data collected through online questionnaires, patient feedback from interviews held during their follow-up visits
and feedback from the patient representative and PAG meetings will be analysed and collated by the study
o
academic fellow and the reports will be presented during the regular TMG meetings. Patients’ views obtained
through these regular PPI activities will be used to directly shape the trial throughout its lifecycle. It will be used to
i
improve the trial design, the recruitment and retention of study participants, identify outcome measures for the
s
definitive trial, decide whether a sham procedure should be included, how to disseminate the results and
communicate with the patients and public. It will be a keystone in designing the definitive trial.
s
Participants of the study will be interviewed during their following visits and asked to provide feedback on their
experience as a trial participants. If any areas of improvement are identified, we will discuss these with the TSC and
i
TMG to further improve the trial design.
m
All PPI members will be supported by the clinical academic fellow and our psychology co-applicant throughout the
study duration to ensure that they understand the trial progression required to make informed decisions and actively
contribute. b
u
Please describe the ways in which patients and the public will be actively involved in the proposed research,
including any training and support provided.
s
Please see the PPI section of the Detailed Research Plan for further details of PPI activities embedded in the trial
design phase. The PETALS trial will be co-produced by VLS patients. Our patient representative will be the PPI Lead
-
and a member of the TMG. She will attend regular meetings to discuss the progression of the trial and troubleshoot
any issues. Our PAG e composed of four women affected by VLS will meet online regularly to discuss possible
changes to the design of the study, the most suitable outcome measures for the definitive trial, the inclusion of a
sham procedure, the most appropriate platform to disseminate the results and communicate with a wider group of
r
VLS patients.
P
Members of the PAG and the patient representative will review the Patient Information Sheets and Consent Forms
and will help us produce patient educational materials explaining the trial design and the proposed treatments. They
will be consulted if any barriers to recruitment or patient retention are identified to identify potential solutions.
We will ask all eligible patients who declined participation to complete a questionnaire on the reasons for their
unwillingness to participate to identify possible areas of improvement. We will also contact all patients who dropped
out of the study and ask for their feedback. Participants of the study will be interviewed during their following visits
and asked to provide feedback on their experience as trial participants. If any areas of improvement are identified,
we will discuss these with the TSC and TMG to further improve the trial design.
All participants will be asked about their willingness to participate if a sham procedure was included after
randomisation and after surgery.
We will continue to engage with a wider group of national and international VLS patients on Facebook support
groups through online questionnaires on the most suitable outcome measures for the definitive trial, sham
procedure, the most appropriate platform to disseminate the results among a wider group of VLS patients and ways
to increase the awareness of the study.
The PPI will form a keystone in the preparation and launch of the definitive trial.
In rare cases where proposals do NOT involve patients and the public, clear justification must be provided.

award_meta.application_title: Lichen Sclerosus – the PETALS multicentre randomised controlled feasibility

plain_english_summary.text:
Background
Vulval lichen sclerosus (VLS) is an itchy condition affecting the skin around the entrance to the vagina (vulva). In
severe cases, it causes scarring and fusion of parts of the vulva, leading to painful skin tears, difficulty in having sex
and going to the toilet. VLS affects women of any age but is most diagnosed before puberty and after menopause.
Treatment includes lifelong use of strong corticosteroid creams, which are effective for most women. For most
patients the condition is controlled by this treatment but some experience ongoing symptoms, scarring and skin
thickening. Recent studies have introduced two potential surgical treatments: fat grafting and platelet-rich plasma
which may improve VLS symptoms and reduce scarring. Fat grafting involves collecting fat from the patient’s own
body and injecting it into the vulva. Platelet-rich plasma is obtained from the patient's blood, prepared in a special
way, and then injected into the vulva.
Aims n
We want to carry out a feasibility (pilot) study in which patients will either undergo one of these new treatments or
o
use corticosteroid cream only to check if these treatments are acceptable and how to best measure their
effectiveness. We also want to know if it is possible to perform a larger trial in multiple hospitals in the future to find
out if these treatments work. i
s
Design and methods
s
This study will involve up to 75 women with VLS. They will be allocated randomly and equally into three groups: fat
grafting, platelet-rich plasma and corticosteroid cream only. Patients in the surgical groups will undergo one surgery
i
and will continue using the corticosteroid cream. Participants will complete questionnaires about their symptoms,
m
quality of life and sexual function. We will assess improvements by analysing photographs of the vulva and
measuring the thickness of the vulval skin using specialised equipment. Patients will be followed up for six months.
Patient and public involvement
b
Our patient representative, a woman with VLS, has contributed to the study design. We also conducted an online
survey involving 809 women with VLS u to shape the study. The patient representative will remain on the trial
committee and participate in decision-making throughout the study. We established a patient advisory group of
women with VLS to gather their ongoing opinions and ensure our research aligns with patients’ priorities.
s
Dissemination
-

methods.text:
Using all of the headings in the order presented below, please use this section to clearly explain your proposed
research. Schematics, tables, illustrations, graphs, and other types of graphics can be embedded to clarify the
research plan but they should not clutter the central narrative. Images do not count towards the overall word count
but inclusion of them to overcome word limits is not permitted. Images may only be included within the 'Research
Plan.' Images included in other sections will be removed from the application and not seen by reviewers.
For further information for what is required under each heading please refer to the Guidance for Applicants. As this
is the main part of your application which will be considered by the reviewing committee, you should ensure
that the information is accurate, succinct, clearly laid out and provides sufficient methodological detail.
NOTE: Applicants should aim to reserve a significant proportion of the word count for the project plan to
enable methodological approaches to be fully specified.
1. Background and Rationale
2. Aims and Objectives
3. Research Plan / Methods
4. Dissemination, Outputs and anticipated Impact
5. Project / research timetable
6. Project Management
7. Ethics / Regulatory Approvals
8. Project / Research expertise
9. Success criteria and barriers to proposed work
1. Background and Rationale
What is the problem being addressed?
Vulval Lichen Sclerosus (VLS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease affecting the vulva and anogenital region (1).
The prevalence of VLS in the UK is up to 3% of adult women (2,3), and girls as young as 4-6 n years are also affected
(7-15% of cases) (6,7).
o
VLS aetiology is unknown but is believed to be a combination of genetic susceptibility and environmental factors
triggering an autoimmune reaction which leads to inflammation, tissue injury, dysregulation of fibroblast and collagen
metabolism (1). i
s
VLS symptoms include itching, burning and pain. Typical lesions are flat, porcelain-white spots with erythema,
ecchymosis, hyperkeratosis, and excoriations due to itching (1). Diagnosis is clinically based and supplemented by
s
biopsy, in particular when neoplastic lesions are suspected (1). VLS has a 4-6.7% risk of vulval squamous cell
carcinoma (8,9).
i
m
Like the untreated course in adults, failure to treat prepubertal VLS patients leads to progressive scarring with loss of
architecture in up to 75% of adolescent cases (6). VLS symptoms in girls may also differ from the adult presentation
with girls being more likely to have urinary or bowel symptoms and vulval purpura at first presentation (4).
b
Forty percent of women of reproductive age may be asymptomatic and are commonly misdiagnosed (9,10). Pruritus,
which is the most reported VLS symptom, is more pronounced in hypoestrogenic states and could be the reason
u
why women in the reproductive years experience milder symptoms, possibly contributing to the delay in diagnosis
(9). The time from the reported onset of symptoms until diagnosis is up to five years (4,10). Many cases present late
and are misdiagnosed as candida or other mild vulval disorders (10).
s
There is no cure for VLS and treatments are limited (1). First-line treatment involves lifelong topical corticosteroids
-
(TCS) which are effective in reducing inflammation, development of fibrosis and change in architecture (11).
Previous studies have e shown that less than 67% of patients comply with treatment (12).
A meta-analysis found that TCS are effective in the acute and maintenance phases but are inadequate for reversing
r
the scarring and loss of vulval architecture once it has already occurred (13). The prevalence of vulval fibrosis and
P
scarring in VLS is poorly reported but studies have reported rates as high as 64-79% in adult women (2,4) and 27%
in prepubertal girls (4). A delay in diagnosis of 2 years or less is associated with less scarring on presentation
suggesting that early diagnosis may prevent scarring in some cases (4). Other treatments have limited efficacy
evidence (13).
VLS commonly affects the fourchette, labia minora and majora, clitoral hood, perineum and perianal area.
Dyspareunia caused by fourchette fissuring, erosions and fissures impacts sexual functioning and intimate
relationships (14).
Two surgical treatments that might be effective in reversing VLS-related inflammation, fissuring and fibrosis,
autologous fat transfer (AFG) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP), have limited evidence of clinical efficacy. There are no
high-quality randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing these options against each other and standard care.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients and health care services?
Impact of VLS
The European Commission has acknowledged VLS as a high-burden, under-researched condition, highlighting
insufficient funding relative to the disease burden (15). VLS causes sexual dysfunction manifesting as dyspareunia,
vulvodynia, difficulty achieving orgasm, and loss of sexual interest (14,16). A 2015 British Association of
Dermatologists vulval health survey involving 325 women revealed substantial daily life disruptions, including
difficulties in walking (38%) and sleeping (36%). Sexual function was impaired in 84%, 79% experienced strain on
personal relationships, 89% reported negative effects on emotional wellbeing with 22% reporting thoughts of suicide
or self-harm. Work capacity is diminished in 39% (17).
Improving future health services and VLS care
This study addresses several of the Top 10 priorities of the VLS Priority Setting Partnership (18). If feasibility is
shown, the subsequent RCT will aim to fill the evidence gaps in the surgical management of functional and structural
architectural changes, symptom reduction and improvement of quality of life (QoL). High-quality prospective
evidence is required to develop best practice guidelines to standardise treatment delivery.
The Royal Free Hospital is the only NHS centre providing AFG for VLS, with one centre in Nottingham having done
so in the past. There is a sporadic provision of AFG and PRP privately without an adequate evidence base. There is
no provision of PRP for VLS in the NHS. This study will include Nottingham as a Patient Identification Centre (PIC)
n
and will explore the feasibility of a second treatment centre.
Review of existing evidence – How does the existing literature support this proposal?
o
Systematic reviews of clinical efficacy
i
s
We conducted a systematic review of PRP and AFG as VLS treatments, identifying 15 studies of mostly poor quality
(level 3-4 evidence). These studies demonstrated improvement in VLS signs, symptoms, QoL and sexual function.
One RCT (5), reported as an abstract, assessed PRP in VLS, finding no significant difference between the
s
experimental and control groups. One RCT (19) reported the efficacy of PRP mixed with AFG and found significant
improvements in fissures, itching, dyspareunia, QoL and inflammation. We found, as others did, that heterogeneity of
i
outcome measures hindered meaningful analysis (13). No trials compared PRP vs AFG vs TCS alone.
m
Trial register
Having searched clinical trial databases, we b identified two RTCs evaluating the effectiveness of PRP in VLS
(20,21)(Table 1). There are no currently ongoing studies evaluating AFG in VLS.
u
Table 1. RCT evaluating PRP in VLS.
s
-
e
r
P
Pilot study
We carried out two studies; a retrospective open cohort study and a prospective open cohort study.
The retrospective cohort study evaluating the effectiveness of AFG in VLS (n=59) found significant improvement in
the visual analogue scale for itching and a significant decrease in sexual distress.
The prospective cohort study (n=33) of patients treated with AFG (16). We found that AFG has a potential anti-
inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effect (Figure 1).
Figure 1. Results of the prospective cohort study.
n
o
i
s
s
i
m
b
u
s
-
e
r
Validation of additional outcome measures
P
In this trial, two assessment methods we have previously validated will be used: the Vulvar Architecture Severity
Scale (22) and 3D Volumetric Imaging (23).
Patient and Public Involvement
In line with NIHR Patient and Public Involvement resources, the NIHR Collaboration for Leadership in Applied Health
Research Toolkit and utilising the GRIPP2-SF framework (24) Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) has been
present throughout the trial design and used as a keystone in this feasibility trial.
We worked with our patient co-applicant and a PPI focus group regularly to identify key themes:
 Is present treatment adequate?
 Is this trial necessary and relevant?
 Could we include a sham?
 Barriers to entry and long-term engagement?
 What are the key outcomes we should measure?
The methods used included:
 One-to-one discussions with our patient representative and members of the patient advisory group (PAG)
 Focus group and one-to-one interviews with 18 VLS patients
 Online survey of the trial proposal and preferred outcome measures completed by 809 VLS patients
 Small focus groups and one-to-one interviews with our PPI co-applicant and members of the PAG in
response to NIHR Stage 1 feedback
The initial PPI stage included 18 patients. Patients were invited to a focus group to discuss trial design, the proposed
procedures, the sham procedure, and potential outcome measures. The outcomes tested with the group were
derived from the VLS outcome domains (25). Results are summarised in Figure 2.
Figure 2. PPI focus group results.
n
o
i
s
s
i
m
b
u

-
e
r
P
The online survey was designed in collaboration with our trial consultant clinical psychologist and the patient co-
applicant based on the focus group outcomes. It was disseminated on national and international VLS patient forums
to learn from a wider international group of VLS patients. It was completed by 809 women. The results are
summarised in Figure 3.
Figure 3. PPI online survey results.
n
o
i
s
s
i
m
b
u
s
-
e
r
P
The survey results highlighted the need for the study and the lack of effective treatments for VLS-related fibrosis.
We are committed to involving the patients at all stages of the PETALS trial. We have involved our PPI Lead from
the early stages of the trial and used her advice to shape the trial design. We also created a PAG which consists of
four women with VLS to ensure that patients’ views shape the progression of the PETALS trial. One of the members
of our PAG is a founder of the Lichen Sclerosus and Vulval Cancer Awareness website
(https://www.lsvcukawareness.co.uk) with experience in advocating for women with VLS and vulval cancer and
raising awareness of these conditions. The PAG will advise us on the trial throughout its lifecycle through regular
online meetings, questionnaires, results dissemination and creation of the online platform to increase awareness of
the trial and the proposed treatments.
The Plain English Summary has been reviewed by our patient representative and four members of the PAG. All
jargon has been removed and a clearer explanation of the vulval anatomy, VLS signs and symptoms and their
impact on patients’ lives have been added. All patients agreed that the updated summary is easy to understand.
2. Aims and objectives
What is the research question / aims and objectives?
Our feasibility trial will aim to establish if the progression to the definitive trial is possible. This will be achieved by
answering the following questions:
1. What proportion of eligible patients are willing to be randomised?
2. What is the average number of patients we can recruit per month and the number of patients we can recruit
in total?
3. What is the rate of withdrawal from the study, data collection completeness and the impact of the distance
travelled to the treatment centre on the patient retention rate?
4. Are the treatments acceptable to the patients and clinicians?
5. What is the adherence to the trial protocol and adequate timing of outcome assessments?
6. What is the ideal number of centres required nationally? Can a second treatment centre be set up in
Nottingham?
n
7. What is the optimal measure of efficacy for a multicentre RCT?
8. What is the rate of adverse events?
9. Should a sham procedure be included in the control group in the definitive trial? Is it acceptable to the
o
patients and clinicians?
10. What is the feasibility of collecting quantitative and health economics data and analysis of cost-
i
effectiveness?
s
11. What is the best way to disseminate the results among clinicians and raise awareness of the study and the
proposed interventions?
12. How to disseminate the results among the patients and increase s awareness of the study and the proposed
procedures? What online platform should be used for communicating with patients?
3. Detailed Research Plan and Methods
i
m
Study design
This will be a single-blind (blinded assessors) randomised controlled feasibility trial (Please see
Appendix_CONSORT flow diagram). Following b confirmation of eligibility and written informed consent, participants
will be randomised using a centralised online randomisation system (https://sealedenvelope.com/) to ensure
randomisation concealment and minimise bias. Randomisation and allocation will be overseen by the trial
u
coordinator who will not be involved in the recruitment, treatment or assessment. All patients will be started on a
standardised TCS (Standard of care - ‘SoC’) and vulval care regimen at the baseline visit after randomisation.
s
Randomisation will be 1:1:1 to three treatment arms:
1. SoC alone
-
2. AFG + SoC
e
3. PRP + SoC
The number of patients recruited per month will be one of the feasibility outcomes. We plan to recruit a maximum of
r
75 patients. We outlined the target recruitment per month in Table 4 and the Green criteria has been set as at least 3
patients per month.
P
Patients in two surgical arms will undergo a procedure under general anaesthesia where AFG and PRP are collected
and either AFG or PRP is injected into the vulval area. Patients will be blinded to the injection they receive.
We plan to use unmodified PRP and AFG prepared to a standard protocol injected intraoperatively with the MHRA
who informed us that if used this way, PRP and AFG are acceptable for clinical use and are not Investigational
Medicinal Products (IMP).
A pragmatic approach of allowing patients to continue a potent TCS which they are known to tolerate will be used, as
agreed with dermatology (RS). A maintenance regimen of twice weekly (non-consecutive days) will occur. If patients
experience a flare-up of VLS signs and symptoms an increase will be advised (TCS daily for seven days) as
standard practice.
We will offer a Consultant Dermatologist review as adjuvant management if clinically indicated by the research team
to manage patients whose disease is poorly controlled.
All patients will be followed-up for 6 months.
Health technologies being assessed
AFG is a minimally invasive surgical procedure. Adipose tissue is harvested from the abdomen with a liposuction
cannula, centrifuged and injected into VLS-affected areas.
PRP is obtained from a blood sample which is centrifuged to isolate platelet-rich fraction and injected into the VLS-
affected areas.
Difference between current and planned pathways
Patients in the control arm will continue SoC (standardised TCS regimen), the same as the current NHS SoC. The
interventions include AFG and PRP. AFG is a routine practice at Royal Free Hospital for VLS patients but is not
common practice nationally. PRP is currently not offered for VLS in the NHS.
Setting
Eligible patients will be identified at Royal Free, Nottingham, Southampton and Norwich. The external centres will
refer patients to Royal Free Hospital where they will be randomised, treated and followed-up. To reduce the risk of
n
slow study progression due to NHS waiting times, patients will be operated through a waiting list initiative which will
be factored in the excess treatment costs. To optimise the recruitment rate, Nottingham Hospital will act as a second
Patient Identification Centre (PIC) and refer patients for treatment and follow-up at Royal Free Hospital. We will also
o
receive referrals from Southampton through Dr Helen Lowery and Dr Alice Plant (Dermatology) and from Norwich
through Dr Karen Gibbon (Dermatology).
i
s
We will increase awareness of the trial among patients through our activity on the online VLS patient support groups.
We will continue to collaborate with female vulval health agencies like the British Society for the Study of Vulval
s
Disease (BSSVD) (Please see the Appendix_BSSVD Support Letter) and the Wellbeing of Women (WoW) to
increase awareness of the study among healthcare professionals.
i
m
Summary of patients as research participants
Target population
b
We consulted the NIHR INCLUDE Guidelines (26) to ensure the eligibility criteria allow the inclusion of underserved
patients. We will record age, ethnicity, educational level, employment status and other indicators of underserved
u
patients.
Inclusion criteria s
 Adults (>18 years)
 Female sex
-
Patients who live in the UK

e
 Patients with expert-diagnosed clinical and/or histological diagnosis of VLS
 Able to give informed consent
r
 Able to communicate in English
 P Structural changes secondary to VLS
Exclusion criteria
 Active malignancy or previous diagnosis of intraepithelial neoplasia/carcinoma of the vulva
 Previous vulval AFG or PRP or vulval surgery
 Body mass index <18.5 or >35.0
 Contraindications to general anaesthetic
 Active infection that precludes fat harvest, AFG or PRP injection
 Pregnancy, breastfeeding or planning for pregnancy during the study period
 Connective tissue and autoimmune diseases and diseases which affect the vulva
Screening, eligibility assessment and consent

A copy of the signed consent form will be given to the patient, another placed in the patient’s notes, and the

understand barriers to recruitment.

Statistical Analysis Plan
A CONSORT diagram will be produced to describe the flow of patients.
All feasibility outcomes will be reported using descriptive statistics. Continuous endpoints will be summarised for
baseline characteristics using means and standard deviations (medians and interquartile ranges) per arm.
Categorical and binary variables will be summarised with frequencies and percentages per arm.
Surgical complications will be reported using the Clavien Dindo scale with descriptive statistics. Adverse events will
be tabulated by treatment arm with frequencies and percentages.
The efficacy endpoints will be analysed with linear mixed models to provide the differences in means with 95%
confidence intervals between the surgical and control arms at the two follow-up time points, adjusted for baseline.
Summaries of the regular data reviews will be shared with the oversight committees.

The feasibility study will aim to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost

collecting data to assess the costs for participants and families from a societal perspective.
Within the feasibility study, we will:

 Identify the potential costs of treating side effects following surgical interventions
 Collaborate with the PPI group to adapt the Client Service Receipt Inventory for the study to collect data on

 Identify the main costs for participants and families (e.g., productivity losses)
 Assess participants’ Health-related Quality of life and calculate Quality-Adjusted Life Years (QALYs)
 Perform a literature search to identify economic studies and evidence available on similar interventions (e.g.,
to capture data that might be needed in addition to those collected within the feasibility trial)

participants’ pathway in the three treatment arms and to model costs and effects beyond trial based on up-
to-date UK sources, published literature and clinicians and PPI representatives’ o opinions
In the definitive RCT, a model-based approach will be used to assess cost-effectiveness.

4. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
What do you intend to produce from your research?
The results will inform the design of a definitive trial aiming to assess the efficacy of AFG and PRP as treatments for
VLS. This feasibility trial will allow us to assess the primary and secondary outcomes and the most suitable
assessment tools, calculate the sample size for an adequately powered trial and decide on the number of required
treatment centres. We will also assess the feasibility of performing quantitative and health economics data analysis.
We will identify any barriers to setting up more treatment centres.
We will also assess the acceptability of the trial design to patients and clinicians, in particular the acceptability of
including a sham arm.
One of the feasibility outcomes will be to assess the best way of communicating with patients to disseminate the
results of the study and increase awareness of the trial and proposed procedures. We will set up an online platform
of patients’ choice.
We will present the outcomes of this feasibility study at national and international academic dermatology,
gynaecology and plastic surgery conferences.

n

o

If this feasibility trial is successful, we will apply for the NIHR Health Technology Assessment funding for the
definitive trial as well as for the grants offered by the WoW and LifeArc charities.
What are the possible barriers for further research, development, adoption and implementation?
The barriers to research include the funding and a limited number of agencies we can approach as VLS is an under-
researched high-burden disease.
The barriers to the adoption of the proposed procedures include the need for training and a limited number of
practitioners able to currently provide the treatments. We further assess this in this feasibility trial and identify ways
to share our expertise and set up other treatment centres.
The widespread implementation of the proposed treatments may be limited by the level of evidence produced by our
study which will depend on our ability to recruit enough patients to evaluate the effectiveness of the treatments. It will
also rely on the commissioning groups such as local Care Quality Commission groups and the National Institute for

Patients: The treatment options for VLS are very limited, especially for VLS-related structural changes. If AFG or
PRP are shown to be efficacious in improving VLS signs and symptoms, they may be implemented as a standard
surgical treatment. Additionally, sexual dysfunction and VLS-related changes are significant mental health burdens
on the patients and identifying new effective treatments may result in improved quality of life. Data gathered in our

life-changing to patients and would positively impact their self-esteem, intimate relationships and quality of life.
Public wellbeing and Health economics analysis: Severe VLS is associated with a significant functional impairment
affecting patients’ sleep, daily activities, and work. If the VLS symptoms were reduced for these patients, society
would benefit due to a reduction of societal opportunity costs from the loss of productivity and unemployment. This
feasibility study will allow us to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost

research. Schematics, tables, illustrations, graphs, and other types of graphics can be embedded to clarify the
research plan but they should not clutter the central narrative. Images do not count towards the overall word count
but inclusion of them to overcome word limits is not permitted. Images may only be included within the 'Research
Plan.' Images included in other sections will be removed from the application and not seen by reviewers.
For further information for what is required under each heading please refer to the Guidance for Applicants. As this
is the main part of your application which will be considered by the reviewing committee, you should ensure
that the information is accurate, succinct, clearly laid out and provides sufficient methodological detail.
NOTE: Applicants should aim to reserve a significant proportion of the word count for the project plan to
enable methodological approaches to be fully specified.
1. Background and Rationale
2. Aims and Objectives
3. Research Plan / Methods
4. Dissemination, Outputs and anticipated Impact

6. Project Management

9. Success criteria and barriers to proposed work
1. Background and Rationale
What is the problem being addressed?
Vulval Lichen Sclerosus (VLS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease affecting the vulva and anogenital region (1).
The prevalence of VLS in the UK is up to 3% of adult women (2,3), and girls as young as 4-6 n years are also affected
(7-15% of cases) (6,7).
o
VLS aetiology is unknown but is believed to be a combination of genetic susceptibility and environmental factors
triggering an autoimmune reaction which leads to inflammation, tissue injury, dysregulation of fibroblast and collagen
metabolism (1). i
s
VLS symptoms include itching, burning and pain. Typical lesions are flat, porcelain-white spots with erythema,
ecchymosis, hyperkeratosis, and excoriations due to itching (1). Diagnosis is clinically based and supplemented by
s
biopsy, in particular when neoplastic lesions are suspected (1). VLS has a 4-6.7% risk of vulval squamous cell
carcinoma (8,9).
i
m
Like the untreated course in adults, failure to treat prepubertal VLS patients leads to progressive scarring with loss of
architecture in up to 75% of adolescent cases (6). VLS symptoms in girls may also differ from the adult presentation
with girls being more likely to have urinary or bowel symptoms and vulval purpura at first presentation (4).
b
Forty percent of women of reproductive age may be asymptomatic and are commonly misdiagnosed (9,10). Pruritus,
which is the most reported VLS symptom, is more pronounced in hypoestrogenic states and could be the reason
u
why women in the reproductive years experience milder symptoms, possibly contributing to the delay in diagnosis
(9). The time from the reported onset of symptoms until diagnosis is up to five years (4,10). Many cases present late
and are misdiagnosed as candida or other mild vulval disorders (10).
s
There is no cure for VLS and treatments are limited (1). First-line treatment involves lifelong topical corticosteroids
-
(TCS) which are effective in reducing inflammation, development of fibrosis and change in architecture (11).
Previous studies have e shown that less than 67% of patients comply with treatment (12).
A meta-analysis found that TCS are effective in the acute and maintenance phases but are inadequate for reversing
r
the scarring and loss of vulval architecture once it has already occurred (13). The prevalence of vulval fibrosis and
P
scarring in VLS is poorly reported but studies have reported rates as high as 64-79% in adult women (2,4) and 27%
in prepubertal girls (4). A delay in diagnosis of 2 years or less is associated with less scarring on presentation
suggesting that early diagnosis may prevent scarring in some cases (4). Other treatments have limited efficacy
evidence (13).
VLS commonly affects the fourchette, labia minora and majora, clitoral hood, perineum and perianal area.
Dyspareunia caused by fourchette fissuring, erosions and fissures impacts sexual functioning and intimate
relationships (14).
Two surgical treatments that might be effective in reversing VLS-related inflammation, fissuring and fibrosis,
autologous fat transfer (AFG) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP), have limited evidence of clinical efficacy. There are no
high-quality randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing these options against each other and standard care.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients and health care services?
Impact of VLS
The European Commission has acknowledged VLS as a high-burden, under-researched condition, highlighting
insufficient funding relative to the disease burden (15). VLS causes sexual dysfunction manifesting as dyspareunia,
vulvodynia, difficulty achieving orgasm, and loss of sexual interest (14,16). A 2015 British Association of
Dermatologists vulval health survey involving 325 women revealed substantial daily life disruptions, including
difficulties in walking (38%) and sleeping (36%). Sexual function was impaired in 84%, 79% experienced strain on
personal relationships, 89% reported negative effects on emotional wellbeing with 22% reporting thoughts of suicide
or self-harm. Work capacity is diminished in 39% (17).
Improving future health services and VLS care
This study addresses several of the Top 10 priorities of the VLS Priority Setting Partnership (18). If feasibility is
shown, the subsequent RCT will aim to fill the evidence gaps in the surgical management of functional and structural
architectural changes, symptom reduction and improvement of quality of life (QoL). High-quality prospective
evidence is required to develop best practice guidelines to standardise treatment delivery.
The Royal Free Hospital is the only NHS centre providing AFG for VLS, with one centre in Nottingham having done
so in the past. There is a sporadic provision of AFG and PRP privately without an adequate evidence base. There is
no provision of PRP for VLS in the NHS. This study will include Nottingham as a Patient Identification Centre (PIC)
n
and will explore the feasibility of a second treatment centre.

Study design
This will be a single-blind (blinded assessors) randomised controlled feasibility trial (Please see
Appendix_CONSORT flow diagram). Following b confirmation of eligibility and written informed consent, participants
will be randomised using a centralised online randomisation system (https://sealedenvelope.com/) to ensure
randomisation concealment and minimise bias. Randomisation and allocation will be overseen by the trial
u
coordinator who will not be involved in the recruitment, treatment or assessment. All patients will be started on a
standardised TCS (Standard of care - ‘SoC’) and vulval care regimen at the baseline visit after randomisation.
s
Randomisation will be 1:1:1 to three treatment arms:
1. SoC alone
-
2. AFG + SoC
e
3. PRP + SoC
The number of patients recruited per month will be one of the feasibility outcomes. We plan to recruit a maximum of
r
75 patients. We outlined the target recruitment per month in Table 4 and the Green criteria has been set as at least 3
patients per month.
P
Patients in two surgical arms will undergo a procedure under general anaesthesia where AFG and PRP are collected
and either AFG or PRP is injected into the vulval area. Patients will be blinded to the injection they receive.
We plan to use unmodified PRP and AFG prepared to a standard protocol injected intraoperatively with the MHRA
who informed us that if used this way, PRP and AFG are acceptable for clinical use and are not Investigational
Medicinal Products (IMP).
A pragmatic approach of allowing patients to continue a potent TCS which they are known to tolerate will be used, as
agreed with dermatology (RS). A maintenance regimen of twice weekly (non-consecutive days) will occur. If patients
experience a flare-up of VLS signs and symptoms an increase will be advised (TCS daily for seven days) as
standard practice.
We will offer a Consultant Dermatologist review as adjuvant management if clinically indicated by the research team
to manage patients whose disease is poorly controlled.
All patients will be followed-up for 6 months.
Health technologies being assessed
AFG is a minimally invasive surgical procedure. Adipose tissue is harvested from the abdomen with a liposuction
cannula, centrifuged and injected into VLS-affected areas.
PRP is obtained from a blood sample which is centrifuged to isolate platelet-rich fraction and injected into the VLS-
affected areas.
Difference between current and planned pathways
Patients in the control arm will continue SoC (standardised TCS regimen), the same as the current NHS SoC. The
interventions include AFG and PRP. AFG is a routine practice at Royal Free Hospital for VLS patients but is not
common practice nationally. PRP is currently not offered for VLS in the NHS.

Statistical Analysis Plan
A CONSORT diagram will be produced to describe the flow of patients.
All feasibility outcomes will be reported using descriptive statistics. Continuous endpoints will be summarised for
baseline characteristics using means and standard deviations (medians and interquartile ranges) per arm.
Categorical and binary variables will be summarised with frequencies and percentages per arm.
Surgical complications will be reported using the Clavien Dindo scale with descriptive statistics. Adverse events will
be tabulated by treatment arm with frequencies and percentages.
The efficacy endpoints will be analysed with linear mixed models to provide the differences in means with 95%
confidence intervals between the surgical and control arms at the two follow-up time points, adjusted for baseline.
Summaries of the regular data reviews will be shared with the oversight committees.
Health Economics Analysis
The feasibility study will aim to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost
and the potential economic benefit for the NHS, personal and social services. We will explore the feasibility of
collecting data to assess the costs for participants and families from a societal perspective.
Within the feasibility study, we will:
 Identify the main cost components of each surgical intervention (equipment, materials, staff cost)
 Identify the potential costs of treating side effects following surgical interventions
 Collaborate with the PPI group to adapt the Client Service Receipt Inventory for the study to collect data on
participants’ health and social care resources
 Identify the main costs for participants and families (e.g., productivity losses)
 Assess participants’ Health-related Quality of life and calculate Quality-Adjusted Life Years (QALYs)
 Perform a literature search to identify economic studies and evidence available on similar interventions (e.g.,
to capture data that might be needed in addition to those collected within the feasibility trial)
n
 Design a decision model (e.g., Markov model) using data from the feasibility study to describe the
participants’ pathway in the three treatment arms and to model costs and effects beyond trial based on up-
to-date UK sources, published literature and clinicians and PPI representatives’ o opinions
In the definitive RCT, a model-based approach will be used to assess cost-effectiveness.

Sample size
m
Minimum target recruitment of 3 patients per month has been estimated as outlined in the traffic light system (Table

We illustrate the power that this sample (n=75) might provide based on the prospective cohort study (n=33) of VLS
patients treated with AFG (16). Using the Female sexual function index (FSFI), the standard deviation of the pre-post

one of the surgical arms in the study is 4 points, assuming the same standard deviation, a sample size of 25 per arm
provides 80% power at the conventional 2-sided alpha of 5%. This assumption and analysis will also be used to

estimate the correct future sample size as well as determine the appropriateness of this outcome measure in a

future multi-centred trial.

4. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
What do you intend to produce from your research?
The results will inform the design of a definitive trial aiming to assess the efficacy of AFG and PRP as treatments for
VLS. This feasibility trial will allow us to assess the primary and secondary outcomes and the most suitable
assessment tools, calculate the sample size for an adequately powered trial and decide on the number of required
treatment centres. We will also assess the feasibility of performing quantitative and health economics data analysis.
We will identify any barriers to setting up more treatment centres.
We will also assess the acceptability of the trial design to patients and clinicians, in particular the acceptability of
including a sham arm.
One of the feasibility outcomes will be to assess the best way of communicating with patients to disseminate the
results of the study and increase awareness of the trial and proposed procedures. We will set up an online platform
of patients’ choice.
We will present the outcomes of this feasibility study at national and international academic dermatology,
gynaecology and plastic surgery conferences.

What are the possible barriers for further research, development, adoption and implementation?
The barriers to research include the funding and a limited number of agencies we can approach as VLS is an under-
researched high-burden disease.
The barriers to the adoption of the proposed procedures include the need for training and a limited number of
practitioners able to currently provide the treatments. We further assess this in this feasibility trial and identify ways
to share our expertise and set up other treatment centres.
The widespread implementation of the proposed treatments may be limited by the level of evidence produced by our
study which will depend on our ability to recruit enough patients to evaluate the effectiveness of the treatments. It will
also rely on the commissioning groups such as local Care Quality Commission groups and the National Institute for
Health and Care Excellence.
What do you think the impact of your research will be and for whom?
Patients: The treatment options for VLS are very limited, especially for VLS-related structural changes. If AFG or
PRP are shown to be efficacious in improving VLS signs and symptoms, they may be implemented as a standard
surgical treatment. Additionally, sexual dysfunction and VLS-related changes are significant mental health burdens
on the patients and identifying new effective treatments may result in improved quality of life. Data gathered in our
prospective and retrospective studies evaluating AFG in VLS has highlighted that addressing these issues would be
life-changing to patients and would positively impact their self-esteem, intimate relationships and quality of life.
Public wellbeing and Health economics analysis: Severe VLS is associated with a significant functional impairment
affecting patients’ sleep, daily activities, and work. If the VLS symptoms were reduced for these patients, society
would benefit due to a reduction of societal opportunity costs from the loss of productivity and unemployment. This
feasibility study will allow us to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost
and potential benefits for the NHS and personal and societal services. This study may also improve VLS awareness
among the public and might help get funding streams for female vulval disorders.

effective approach will lay a strong foundation for conducting the subsequent definitive RCT through a Clinical Trials
Unit.
Several members of the team have considerable experience managing feasibility randomised controlled surgical
trials, and have been involved in the design, development and writing of this application, providing statistical, trial
management, regulatory and costing advice throughout. Members of the team will also be responsible for data
management, trial set-up and closure, statistical monitoring, and data analysis.
Early in the project, a launch meeting will be held to allow for detailed discussions with all staff to facilitate efficient
site set-up. The day-to-day management of the trial will be carried out by the team at the Royal Free Hospital
London and Nottingham. Data will be collected on a bespoke database (REDCap) kept in a secure environment and
backed up regularly. When data collection is complete, this data will be analysed by the trial statistician according to
a pre-approved Statistical Analysis Plan.
The Trial Management Group (TMG) will consist of the co-applications and will be chaired by Professor Butler. They
will meet regularly (initially weekly) via teleconference (Teams). The PPI co-applicant will be given a briefing before
each meeting to ensure they can understand the terminology, enabling them to contribute effectively.
Independent oversight will be provided by a Trial Steering Committee (TSC) and will consist of people with
appropriate expertise and experience. They will meet by teleconference (Teams) before the first participant is
enrolled, yearly, finishing with a final meeting to discuss the outcomes and analysis. More frequent meetings may be
required if issues arise that need to be discussed, such as changes to the protocol to improve recruitment.
One member of the TSC will be nominated to review safety data and report to the Committee. Any safety concerns
raised during the trial will be investigated by Professor Butler and communicated to both the TSC and Sponsor
(Royal Free Hospital London).
7. Ethics/ Regulatory approvals
If the trial funding is granted, the trial protocol will be finalised, and the application submitted to the ethics committee
before the funding has commenced to avoid potential delays. As described in point 1 of the Response to Stage 1
n
section, the MHRA’s approval is not required for this feasibility trial.
8. Project/ Research Expertise
o
Professor Norman Williams is a methodologist with more than 25 years of experience running clinical trials at UCL.
i
He specialises in “surgeon-led” studies, particularly feasibility randomised trials with a pragmatic design. He has
s
authored or co-authored more than 300 publications and teaches trial methodology to MSc degree students. In this
study, he will be involved in the database design, study set-up, coordination of the trial and its conduct and
management, and analysis and write-up.
s
Dr Hakim-Moulay Dehbi is head of statistics at the Comprehensive Clinical Trials Unit at UCL. He has a wealth of
i
experience in clinical trials in a variety of disease areas, including cardiovascular diseases, ophthalmology,
m
oncology, infectious diseases and neonatology among others. His main methodological interest is the design of
early-phase clinical trials. He has published extensively on this topic and is well-cited.
Miss Monica Panca is a senior health economist b at the Comprehensive Clinical Trials Unit at UCL. She is involved in
numerous clinical trials in different disease areas including mental health, cardiovascular diseases, neonatology, and
ophthalmology. Her methodological interest is in decision modelling using different modelling techniques. She has
u
published extensively in peer-reviewed journals.
Dr Esther Hansen is a Consultant s Clinical Psychologist and Trust Lead for Psychological Therapies, with 20 years of
clinical experience of working with patients with long-term health conditions or those undergoing surgery. She
provides psychological interventions to patients with vulval lichen sclerosus, to help with symptom management, and
-
improve their quality of life, emotional wellbeing and sexual functioning. She has expertise in running patient focus
e
groups, qualitative analysis and engaging patients as part of service development and in research. She has
published in peer-reviewed medical and psychology journals.
r
Miss Aurora Almadori is a Plastic Surgeon with a specialist interest in Vulval Reconstruction and Regenerative
P
Surgery. She has recently completed a PhD on the use of fat grafting to treat dermal fibrosis and scarring at
UCL. She has received 14 prizes and awards for her research. Miss Almadori has published more than twenty
scientific publications in peer-reviewed journals and has contributed to five textbooks of plastic surgery.
Rosalind is a clinical academic with a specific interest in women’s health and vulval disease. She is an NIHR
Advanced Fellow and current projects revolve around the accurate diagnosis, long-term treatment, and core
outcomes in lichen sclerosus. In particular, she is the lead for the NIHR HTA-funded ‘PEARLS’ multicentre
randomised controlled trial. Rosalind is the research lead for the British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease, a
Fellow of the International Society for the Study of Vulvovaginal Disease and co-lead for the Patient Advisors for
Vulval Research (PAVE) group in Nottingham.
Dr Victoria Swale is a Consultant Dermatologist and Dermatopathologist at Royal Free Hospital with 20 years of
experience. She specialises in vulval diseases. Dr Swale is a member of the British Society for the Study of Vulval
Disease, the British Society of Dermatologists and the Honorary Secretary of the British Association of
Dermatologists.
Dr Benjamin Langridge is a Specialist Registrar in Plastic Surgery at the Royal Free Hospital. He has extensive
clinical experience in the use of AFG and PRP in the treatment of fibrotic diseases, including VLS. He has completed
an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, MSc degree, and his PhD research on the treatment of fibrotic diseases with
AFG. He has published widely and co-ordinates the NIHR-funded Sys-Stem feasibility study investigating AFG as a
treatment for orofacial fibrosis in Systemic Sclerosis.
Ms Sara Jasionowska is a plastic surgery trainee at Royal Free Hospital. She has led the PPI component of this
study and has carried out literature reviews of the outcome assessments in VLS and the effectiveness of AFG and
PRP. She is a co-applicant on the NIHR-funded HTA GRACE trial where she was responsible for liaising with
peripheral sites and the PPI work.

Justification of costs
s
Monitoring Visits:
3x tickets at £100 per annum: Necessary for project monitoring and ensuring the effective implementation of
-
activities. These visits facilitate real-time observation, feedback, and collaboration.
e
3x £20 subsistence per visit, per annum: Covers essential expenses related to meals and incidentals during
monitoring visits, ensuring the well-being of staff and the success of visits.
3x overnight hotel stays per annum: Required for monitoring visits that extend beyond a single day, ensuring
r
sufficient time for comprehensive assessments.
P
National Conferences:
Conference fees for 2 participants per annum £350: Supports professional development, knowledge sharing, and
networking opportunities crucial for project success.
National travel ticket £100 x 2 conference participants per annum: Covers travel expenses for participation,
promoting collaboration and dissemination of project findings.
2 x 2 overnight stays per conference participant per annum: Ensures participants can fully engage in conference
activities by accommodating overnight stays when necessary.
£100 per day, for 2 days, per conference participant, per annum: Covers additional expenses associated with
attending conferences, such as meals and incidental costs.
International Conference:
2 attendees for international conference: Enables global exposure, fostering cross-cultural collaborations and
knowledge exchange.
2 attendees for international conference flights: Essential for participation in international forums, broadening the
project's impact and visibility.
2 attendees for international conference accommodation (2 days): Ensures the comfort and safety of attendees
during international events.
2 attendees for international conference subsistence (2 days): Covers essential daily expenses for participants
during international conferences.
Patient Involvement:
30x patient reimbursement @ £20: Provides fair compensation to patients for their time and effort, incentivizing their
continued participation in the project from recruitment to completion.
Patient Representative Meetings:
£120 x 36 meetings: Compensates patient representatives for their valuable time and insights, promoting sustained
engagement and commitment to the project.
1 per year, 2 in the last year: Supports ongoing collaboration and ensures representation of patient perspectives
throughout the project's duration.
Allocating £65 per day return ticket for 10 participants (Nottingham to London) fosters in-person collaboration in
hybrid meetings, enhancing engagement. A £15 provision per in-person participant ensures a conducive
environment with tea and coffee, supporting a comfortable and collaborative atmosphere for those physically
present.
Allocating £450 for a dedicated patient helpline ensures effective communication.
Three archiving boxes at £519 each support secure document storage.
£550 per study year for printing and stationery guarantees the production of accurate study materials.
A standard study registration fee of £360 covers essential administrative costs, ensuring compliance and bolstering
the study's credibility.

Justification of costs
s
Monitoring Visits:
3x tickets at £100 per annum: Necessary for project monitoring and ensuring the effective implementation of
-
activities. These visits facilitate real-time observation, feedback, and collaboration.
e
3x £20 subsistence per visit, per annum: Covers essential expenses related to meals and incidentals during
monitoring visits, ensuring the well-being of staff and the success of visits.
3x overnight hotel stays per annum: Required for monitoring visits that extend beyond a single day, ensuring
r
sufficient time for comprehensive assessments.
P
National Conferences:
Conference fees for 2 participants per annum £350: Supports professional development, knowledge sharing, and
networking opportunities crucial for project success.
National travel ticket £100 x 2 conference participants per annum: Covers travel expenses for participation,
promoting collaboration and dissemination of project findings.
2 x 2 overnight stays per conference participant per annum: Ensures participants can fully engage in conference
activities by accommodating overnight stays when necessary.
£100 per day, for 2 days, per conference participant, per annum: Covers additional expenses associated with
attending conferences, such as meals and incidental costs.
International Conference:
2 attendees for international conference: Enables global exposure, fostering cross-cultural collaborations and
knowledge exchange.
2 attendees for international conference flights: Essential for participation in international forums, broadening the
project's impact and visibility.
2 attendees for international conference accommodation (2 days): Ensures the comfort and safety of attendees
during international events.
2 attendees for international conference subsistence (2 days): Covers essential daily expenses for participants
during international conferences.
Patient Involvement:
30x patient reimbursement @ £20: Provides fair compensation to patients for their time and effort, incentivizing their
continued participation in the project from recruitment to completion.
Patient Representative Meetings:
£120 x 36 meetings: Compensates patient representatives for their valuable time and insights, promoting sustained
engagement and commitment to the project.
1 per year, 2 in the last year: Supports ongoing collaboration and ensures representation of patient perspectives
throughout the project's duration.
Allocating £65 per day return ticket for 10 participants (Nottingham to London) fosters in-person collaboration in
hybrid meetings, enhancing engagement. A £15 provision per in-person participant ensures a conducive
environment with tea and coffee, supporting a comfortable and collaborative atmosphere for those physically
present.
Allocating £450 for a dedicated patient helpline ensures effective communication.
Three archiving boxes at £519 each support secure document storage.
£550 per study year for printing and stationery guarantees the production of accurate study materials.
A standard study registration fee of £360 covers essential administrative costs, ensuring compliance and bolstering
the study's credibility.

n

Statistician (5% FTE)
o

Consultant Dermatologists at Nottingham (2.5% FTE) and at Royal Free (1% FTE)
i
Consultant Clinical Psychologist (1% FTE)
s
Consultant Plastic Surgeon (4% FTE)
Clinical Research Fellows (10% FTE and 5% FTE)
